actions.name: Over the past several years, we've written a ton of Dart cod
actions.description: Over the past several years, we've written a ton of Dart code and learned a lot about what works well and what doesn't. We're sharing this with you so

actions.name: 1.  Be consistent. When it comes to things like formatting,
actions.description: 1.  Be consistent. When it comes to things like formatting, and casing, arguments about which is better are subjective and impossible to resolve. What

actions.name: 2.  Be brief. Dart was designed to be familiar, so it inheri
actions.description: 2.  Be brief. Dart was designed to be familiar, so it inherits many of the same statements and expressions as C, Java, JavaScript and other languages.

actions.name: Style – This defines the rules for laying out and organizing
actions.description: Style – This defines the rules for laying out and organizing code, or at least the parts that `dart format` doesn't handle for you. The style topic al

actions.name: Documentation – This tells you everything you need to know a
actions.description: Documentation – This tells you everything you need to know about what goes inside comments. Both doc comments and regular, run-of-the-mill code commen

actions.name: Usage – This teaches you how to make the best use of languag
actions.description: Usage – This teaches you how to make the best use of language features to implement behavior. If it's in a statement or expression, it's covered here.

actions.name: Design – This is the softest topic, but the one with the wid
actions.description: Design – This is the softest topic, but the one with the widest scope. It covers what we've learned about designing consistent, usable APIs for librar

actions.name: Each topic is broken into a few sections. Sections contain a
actions.description: Each topic is broken into a few sections. Sections contain a list of guidelines. Each guideline starts with one of these words:

actions.name: DO name packages, directories, and source files using `lower
actions.description: DO name packages, directories, and source files using `lowercase_with_underscores`.

actions.name: DO place `dart:` imports before other imports.
actions.description: DO place `dart:` imports before other imports.

actions.name: DO place `package:` imports before relative imports.
actions.description: DO place `package:` imports before relative imports.

actions.name: DO specify exports in a separate section after all imports.
actions.description: DO specify exports in a separate section after all imports.

actions.name: DO format your code using `dart format`.
actions.description: DO format your code using `dart format`.

actions.name: CONSIDER changing your code to make it more formatter-friend
actions.description: CONSIDER changing your code to make it more formatter-friendly.

rules.description: If two pieces of code look different it should be because they *are* different in some meaningful way. When a bit of code stands out and catches your
If there are multiple ways to say something, you should generally pick the most concise one. This is not to say you should `code golf` yourself into c
DO guidelines describe practices that should always be followed. There will almost never be a valid reason to stray from them.
DON'T guidelines are the converse: things that are almost never a good idea. Hopefully, we don't have as many of these as other languages do because w
PREFER guidelines are practices that you *should* follow. However, there may be circumstances where it makes sense to do otherwise. Just make sure you
AVOID guidelines are the dual to "prefer": stuff you shouldn't do but where there may be good reasons to on rare occasions.
This sounds like the police are going to beat down your door if you don't have your laces tied correctly. Things aren't that bad. Most of the guidelin
DON'T use a leading underscore for identifiers that aren't private.

directives.description: Best practices recommended by the Dart and Flutter teams. These instructions were taken from [Effective Dart](https://dart.dev/effective-dart) and [Ar
We split the guidelines into a few separate topics for easy digestion:
CONSIDER guidelines are practices that you might or might not want to follow, depending on circumstances, precedents, and your own preference.
Some guidelines describe an exception where the rule does *not* apply. When listed, the exceptions may not be exhaustive—you might still need to use y
DO capitalize acronyms and abbreviations longer than two letters like words.
PREFER using wildcards for unused callback parameters.
DO use curly braces for all flow control statements.
DO use `///` doc comments to document members and types.
DO separate the first sentence of a doc comment into its own paragraph.
PREFER a noun phrase or non-imperative verb phrase for a function or method if returning a value is its primary purpose.
